steps.reveal: Omnis = all (acc. masc. pl., archaic for 'omnes')

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would typically be 'omnes' — here 'omnis' is accusative plural (archaic -is for -es)
hints.genitive: Genitive singular would also be 'omnis', but here it modifies 'homines' as accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'omnibus' — 'omnis' is accusative plural here

hints.nominative: While 'homines' can be nominative plural, here it is accusative — the subject of the infinitive 'praestare'
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'hominum' — 'homines' is accusative here
hints.vocative: Vocative plural would be 'homines' (same form), but Sallust is not addressing anyone

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'hominis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'hominis' ends in -is

hints.demonstrative pronoun: 'Sese' is a doubled form of 'se' for emphasis — not a demonstrative
hints.relative pronoun: Relative pronoun would be 'qui/quae/quod' — 'sese' is reflexive

hints.to study: While English 'study' derives from it, 'studere' in Latin means 'to be eager/strive for'
hints.to stand: That would be 'stare' — 'student' comes from 'studere' (to be eager/strive)
hints.to be foolish: That would be 'stultum esse' — 'studere' means 'to be eager/strive'

hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'studeant' — 'student' with -ent is present indicative (2nd conj.)
hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'studuerunt' — 'student' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'studebunt' — 'student' is present indicative

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand)
hints.struo, struere, struxi, structum: These are the parts of 'struere' (to build)
hints.stupeo, stupere, stupui: These are the parts of 'stupere' (to be stunned)

hints.to present: That would be 'praesentare' — 'praestare' means 'to surpass/excel' here
hints.to stand before: While etymologically related (prae + stare), 'praestare' means 'to surpass/excel'
hints.to provide: 'Praestare' can mean 'to provide', but with the dative 'ceteris animalibus' it means 'to surpass'

hints.present indicative: Present indicative would be 'praestat' — 'praestare' ending in -re is infinitive
hints.imperfect subjunctive: Imperfect subjunctive would be 'praestaret' — 'praestare' is infinitive
hints.future infinitive: Future infinitive would be 'praestaturum esse' — 'praestare' is present infinitive

hints.sto, stare, steti, statum: These are the parts of 'stare' (to stand) — 'praestare' is its compound prae + stare
hints.praesideo, praesidere, praesedi, praeses: These are the parts of 'praesidere' (to preside/guard)
hints.praesum, praeesse, praefui: These are the parts of 'praeesse' (to be in charge)

steps.reveal: ceteris = to other/the rest of (dat. pl.)

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'ceteri' — 'ceteris' with -is is dative/ablative plural
hints.ablative: While 'ceteris' could be ablative, with 'praestare' (surpass) it takes dative of comparison
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'ceterorum' — 'ceteris' is dative here

hints.nominative: Nominative plural would be 'animalia' — 'animalibus' with -ibus is dative/ablative
hints.ablative: While the form is the same, 'animalibus' is dative with 'praestare' (surpass + dative)
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'animalium' — 'animalibus' is dative here

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'animalis' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'animalis' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'animalis' ends in -is

translation: All men who strive to surpass other animals
